 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-1mv","title":"P2.2 Indexer multi-root orchestration","description":"# Indexer Multi-Root Orchestration

## Context
The indexer needs to build a list of scan roots and orchestrate scanning across them, injecting provenance appropriately.

## Location
src/indexer/mod.rs

## Current Flow
1. Build single ScanContext with data_dir
2. For each connector, call scan()
3. Persist results

## New Flow
1. Build list of scan roots:
   - Local roots (from watch_roots() or equivalent)
   - Remote mirror roots (from sources table)
2. Group roots by connector affinity
3. For each connector:
   - For each relevant root:
     - Create ScanContext with that root
     - Call scan()
     - Inject provenance into results
     - Persist with source_id

## Implementation

### Build Scan Roots List
\`\`\`rust
fn build_scan_roots(storage: &SqliteStorage) -> Vec<ScanRoot> {
    let mut roots = Vec::new();
    
    // Add local roots (existing logic from watch_roots())
    for local_root in local_default_roots() {
        roots.push(ScanRoot {
            path: local_root,
            origin: Origin::local(),
            platform: Some(Platform::current()),
            workspace_rewrites: vec![],
        });
    }
    
    // Add remote mirror roots
    for source in storage.list_sources()? {
        if source.kind == SourceKind::Ssh {
            let mirror_root = data_dir.join("remotes").join(&source.id).join("mirror");
            if mirror_root.exists() {
                roots.push(ScanRoot {
                    path: mirror_root,
                    origin: Origin {
                        source_id: source.id.clone(),
                        kind: source.kind,
                        host: source.host_label.clone(),
                    },
                    platform: source.platform,
                    workspace_rewrites: parse_rewrites(&source.config),
                });
            }
        }
    }
    
    roots
}
\`\`\`

### Connector Affinity
Not all roots apply to all connectors. For example:
- ClaudeCodeConnector cares about ~/.claude or remotes/*/mirror/**/.claude
- AiderConnector cares about .aider.chat.history.md anywhere

For now: let connectors handle root relevance via their detect() logic.

### Provenance Injection
After connector.scan() returns:
\`\`\`rust
for conv in conversations.iter_mut() {
    conv.metadata["cass"] = json!({
        "origin": {
            "source_id": scan_root.origin.source_id,
            "kind": scan_root.origin.kind.as_str(),
            "host": scan_root.origin.host,
        }
    });
}
\`\`\`

## Dependencies
- P2.1 (ScanContext extended)
- P1.3 (storage accepts source_id)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Indexer builds list of scan roots
- [ ] Local roots work as before
- [ ] Provenance injected into metadata
- [ ] Results persisted with correct source_id
- [ ] Tests verify multi-root behavior","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","created_at":"2025-12-16T05:57:31.805169Z","updated_at":"2025-12-16T16:33:11.166283Z","closed_at":"2025-12-16T16:33:11.166283Z","close_reason":"Implemented multi-root orchestration with build_scan_roots(), inject_provenance(), and provenance extraction in persist::map_to_internal(). All 294 tests pass.","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-1mv","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-1v7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-16T05:58:17.128097Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-1tmi","title":"[Task] Opt 7.4: Benchmark SQLite ID caching","description":"## Objective
Benchmark the performance improvement from SQLite ID caching.

## Benchmark Scenarios

### 1. Indexing Throughput
```rust
#[bench]
fn bench_indexing_with_cache(b: &mut Bencher) {
    let corpus = generate_corpus(3000);
    b.iter(|| {
        // Cache enabled (default)
        index_corpus(&corpus)
    });
}

#[bench]
fn bench_indexing_without_cache(b: &mut Bencher) {
    std::env::set_var("CASS_SQLITE_CACHE", "0");
    let corpus = generate_corpus(3000);
    b.iter(|| {
        index_corpus(&corpus)
    });
    std::env::remove_var("CASS_SQLITE_CACHE");
}
```

### 2. Syscall Profiling
```bash
# With cache disabled
CASS_SQLITE_CACHE=0 strace -c cargo test -- test_index_corpus

# With cache enabled
strace -c cargo test -- test_index_corpus
```

Compare:
- pwrite64 count
- pread64 count
- Total syscall count

### 3. Cache Hit Ratio
Log cache hits/misses during indexing to verify effectiveness:
- Expected: >90% hit ratio for agent_ids
- Expected: >80% hit ratio for workspace_ids

## Success Criteria
- Indexing time reduction (measure actual %)
- SQLite syscalls reduced by 30-50%
- No memory overhead concerns (cache is small)

## Parent Feature

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-1v7","title":"P2.1 Extend ScanContext with scan roots","description":"# Extend ScanContext with Multi-Root Support

## Context
Currently ScanContext has just data_root (single path) and since_ts. For remote support, we need multiple scan roots, each with provenance.

## Location
src/connectors/mod.rs (ScanContext definition)
src/indexer/mod.rs (ScanContext usage)

## Current Structure
\`\`\`rust
pub struct ScanContext {
    pub data_root: PathBuf,
    pub since_ts: Option<i64>,
}
\`\`\`

## New Structure

### Option A: Add scan_roots alongside data_root (backward compatible)
\`\`\`rust
pub struct ScanContext {
    /// Primary data directory (cass internal state)
    pub data_dir: PathBuf,
    
    /// Scan roots to search for agent logs
    /// If empty, connectors use their default detection logic
    pub scan_roots: Vec<ScanRoot>,
    
    /// High-water mark for incremental indexing
    pub since_ts: Option<i64>,
}

pub struct ScanRoot {
    /// Path to scan (e.g., ~/.claude, or /data/remotes/work-laptop/mirror/home/.claude)
    pub path: PathBuf,
    
    /// Provenance for conversations found under this root
    pub origin: Origin,
    
    /// Optional platform hint (affects path interpretation)
    pub platform: Option<Platform>,
    
    /// Optional path rewrite rules (src_prefix -> dst_prefix)
    pub workspace_rewrites: Vec<(String, String)>,
}
\`\`\`

### Option B: Keep ScanContext simple, pass provenance separately
Keep ScanContext as-is, but indexer tracks provenance externally and injects after scan.

## Recommendation
Option A is cleaner - ScanRoot bundles everything needed for a scan pass.

## Migration Path for Connectors
Most connectors currently check ctx.data_root for tests/overrides. Update them to:
1. If scan_roots is non-empty, iterate over matching roots
2. If scan_roots is empty, use existing default detection logic (maintains backward compat)

## Dependencies
- P1.1 (Origin type)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ScanContext extended with scan_roots
- [ ] ScanRoot struct defined with provenance
- [ ] Backward compatibility: empty scan_roots = old behavior
- [ ] At least one connector updated to use new structure
- [ ] Tests pass","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","created_at":"2025-12-16T05:57:06.602228Z","updated_at":"2025-12-16T15:36:13.711982Z","closed_at":"2025-12-16T15:36:13.711982Z","close_reason":"Added ScanRoot struct with origin, platform, workspace_rewrites. Extended ScanContext with scan_roots vec and data_dir. Connectors updated to use data_dir. Backward compatible via local_default() and use_default_detection(). 5 new unit tests.","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-1v7","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-bfk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-16T05:58:11.903856Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-1wnh","title":"Add CI validation for E2E logging compliance","description":"## Priority 2: Enhance CI Validation for E2E Logging Compliance

### Current State
- scripts/validate-e2e-jsonl.sh ALREADY EXISTS for JSONL validation
- scripts/validate_ci.sh ALREADY EXISTS for general CI checks
- Missing: E2E test file compliance check (PhaseTracker usage)

### Required Changes

1. **Add E2E logging compliance check to validate_ci.sh:**

Add a new check section:
```bash
# ============================================================
# E2E Logging Compliance Check
# ============================================================
echo "X. Checking E2E logging compliance..."

E2E_ERRORS=0

# Check that all e2e_*.rs files import e2e_log module
for f in tests/e2e_*.rs; do
    name=$(basename "$f")
    
    # Check for e2e_log import
    if ! grep -q "use.*e2e_log\|mod.*e2e_log" "$f"; then
        echo "  ERROR: $name missing e2e_log import"
        ((E2E_ERRORS++))
    fi
    
    # Check for PhaseTracker usage (after consolidation is complete)
    if ! grep -q "PhaseTracker" "$f"; then
        echo "  WARNING: $name not using PhaseTracker (may need migration)"
    fi
done

# Check shell scripts
for f in scripts/e2e/*.sh; do
    if [ -f "$f" ] && [ -s "$f" ]; then
        name=$(basename "$f")
        if ! grep -q "e2e_log.sh" "$f"; then
            echo "  WARNING: $name not sourcing e2e_log.sh"
        fi
    fi
done

if [ $E2E_ERRORS -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "  FAILED: $E2E_ERRORS E2E logging compliance error(s)"
    exit 1
fi
echo "  OK: E2E logging compliance checks passed"
```

2. **Add CI workflow step for JSONL validation:**

In .github/workflows/test.yml:
```yaml
- name: Validate E2E JSONL logs
  if: always()
  run: |
    if ls test-results/e2e/*.jsonl 1>/dev/null 2>&1; then
      ./scripts/validate-e2e-jsonl.sh test-results/e2e/*.jsonl
    else
      echo "No JSONL files to validate (tests may have been skipped)"
    fi
```

### Files to Modify
- scripts/validate_ci.sh (add E2E compliance check)
- .github/workflows/test.yml (add JSONL validation step)

### NOTE: DO NOT create scripts/validate_e2e_logging.sh
Use existing validate_ci.sh instead.

### Testing Requirements (CRITICAL)

1. **Test compliance check catches issues:**
```bash
# Create non-compliant file (temporarily)
echo 'fn test() {}' > /tmp/test_e2e_bad.rs
./scripts/validate_ci.sh  # Should warn about missing imports
rm /tmp/test_e2e_bad.rs
```

2. **Test JSONL validation works:**
```bash
# Run tests to generate JSONL
E2E_LOG=1 cargo test --test e2e_search_index -- --nocapture
./scripts/validate-e2e-jsonl.sh test-results/e2e/*.jsonl
echo "Exit code: $?"  # Should be 0
```

3. **Test with invalid JSONL:**
```bash
echo '{"invalid": true}' > test-results/e2e/bad.jsonl
./scripts/validate-e2e-jsonl.sh test-results/e2e/bad.jsonl || echo "Correctly rejected"
rm test-results/e2e/bad.jsonl
```

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] validate_ci.sh checks E2E test file compliance
- [ ] validate_ci.sh warns about missing PhaseTracker usage
- [ ] validate_ci.sh checks shell scripts source e2e_log.sh
- [ ] CI workflow runs validate-e2e-jsonl.sh on JSONL output
- [ ] CI fails if JSONL validation fails
- [ ] Existing validate_ci.sh tests still pass","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T17:22:19.240506348Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T18:06:45.799481289Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-1wnh","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-35pi","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T17:22:41.707890434Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-1wnh","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-3koo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T17:34:45.990962611Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-1wnh","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-yfcu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T17:22:44.006707881Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-etv6","title":"P6.3: Performance Testing & Large Archive Profiling","description":"# P6.3: Performance Testing & Large Archive Profiling

## Overview
NFR-2 specifies performance targets: <3s initial load on 3G, <100ms search after decryption. This task validates those targets across archive sizes and identifies bottlenecks.

## Performance Targets (from PLAN)

| Metric | Target | Measurement |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| Initial page load | <3s on 3G | Lighthouse, WebPageTest |
| Auth page render | <500ms | First Contentful Paint |
| Argon2id derivation | 2-3s desktop, 5-9s mobile | Browser console timing |
| Database decryption | <2s per 10MB | Worker postMessage timing |
| Search latency | <100ms after decryption | FTS5 EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN |
| Virtual scroll FPS | 60fps | Chrome DevTools Performance |
| Memory usage | <500MB for 100K messages | Performance.memory API |

## Test Fixtures

### Archive Size Matrix
```
small:   1,000 messages,   ~2 MB encrypted
medium: 10,000 messages,  ~15 MB encrypted
large:  50,000 messages,  ~70 MB encrypted
xlarge: 100,000 messages, ~150 MB encrypted (near GitHub Pages limit)
```

### Generate Test Archives
```bash
# Generate archives for performance testing
cass pages --export-only ./perf-test-small --since "7 days ago"
cass pages --export-only ./perf-test-medium --since "90 days ago"
cass pages --export-only ./perf-test-large  # All time
```

## Testing Methodology

### 1. Network Profiling (Lighthouse)
```javascript
// Run Lighthouse audit programmatically
const lighthouse = require('lighthouse');
const chromeLauncher = require('chrome-launcher');

async function runLighthouseAudit(url) {
    const chrome = await chromeLauncher.launch({chromeFlags: ['--headless']});
    const options = {
        logLevel: 'info',
        output: 'json',
        port: chrome.port,
        throttling: {
            rttMs: 150,           // 3G simulation
            throughputKbps: 1600, // 3G simulation
            cpuSlowdownMultiplier: 4
        }
    };
    
    const result = await lighthouse(url, options);
    await chrome.kill();
    
    return {
        performance: result.lhr.categories.performance.score * 100,
        fcp: result.lhr.audits['first-contentful-paint'].numericValue,
        lcp: result.lhr.audits['largest-contentful-paint'].numericValue,
        tti: result.lhr.audits['interactive'].numericValue,
        tbt: result.lhr.audits['total-blocking-time'].numericValue,
    };
}
```

### 2. Decryption Timing
```javascript
// Measure decryption performance
async function measureDecryption(archiveUrl) {
    const start = performance.now();
    
    // Load config
    const configStart = performance.now();
    const config = await fetch(`${archiveUrl}/config.json`).then(r => r.json());
    const configTime = performance.now() - configStart;
    
    // Argon2 derivation
    const argonStart = performance.now();
    const kek = await deriveKEK('test-password', config);
    const argonTime = performance.now() - argonStart;
    
    // Download + decrypt chunks
    const decryptStart = performance.now();
    const dbBytes = await downloadAndDecrypt(config);
    const decryptTime = performance.now() - decryptStart;
    
    // Initialize sqlite-wasm
    const sqlStart = performance.now();
    await initDatabase(dbBytes);
    const sqlTime = performance.now() - sqlStart;
    
    return {
        total: performance.now() - start,
        config: configTime,
        argon2: argonTime,
        decrypt: decryptTime,
        sqlite: sqlTime,
        archiveSize: dbBytes.length,
    };
}
```

### 3. Search Performance
```javascript
// Measure FTS5 search latency
async function measureSearchPerformance(db, queries) {
    const results = [];
    
    for (const query of queries) {
        const start = performance.now();
        const result = await db.exec(`
            SELECT m.id, m.content, c.title
            FROM messages_fts 
            JOIN messages m ON messages_fts.rowid = m.id
            JOIN conversations c ON m.conversation_id = c.id
            WHERE messages_fts MATCH ?
            ORDER BY rank
            LIMIT 100
        `, [escapeFts5Query(query)]);
        const elapsed = performance.now() - start;
        
        results.push({
            query,
            elapsed,
            resultCount: result.length,
            ok: elapsed < 100  // Target: <100ms
        });
    }
    
    return results;
}

// Test queries covering different patterns
const TEST_QUERIES = [
    'authentication',           // Common term
    'error handling',           // Two-word phrase
    'async await promise',      // Multi-term
    'react useState hook',      // Technical jargon
    'fix bug',                  // Very common
    'AuthController.ts',        // Code pattern (uses code FTS)
    'sha256',                   // Short term
    'xyzzy123nonexistent',      // No results
];
```

### 4. Memory Profiling
```javascript
// Monitor WASM heap and JS heap
function measureMemoryUsage() {
    const wasmHeap = window.SQL?.Module?.HEAPU8?.length || 0;
    const jsHeap = performance?.memory?.usedJSHeapSize || 0;
    
    return {
        wasmHeapMB: wasmHeap / (1024 * 1024),
        jsHeapMB: jsHeap / (1024 * 1024),
        total: (wasmHeap + jsHeap) / (1024 * 1024),
    };
}

// Track memory during search operations
async function profileSearchMemory(db) {
    const baseline = measureMemoryUsage();
    
    // Run 100 searches
    for (let i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
        await db.exec(`SELECT * FROM messages_fts WHERE messages_fts MATCH 'test' LIMIT 10`);
    }
    
    const after = measureMemoryUsage();
    
    return {
        baseline,
        after,
        leakMB: after.total - baseline.total,
        leakDetected: (after.total - baseline.total) > 10  // >10MB growth = leak
    };
}
```

### 5. Virtual Scroll Performance
```javascript
// Measure scroll frame rate
async function measureScrollPerformance(container) {
    const frames = [];
    let lastTime = performance.now();
    
    const observer = new PerformanceObserver((list) => {
        for (const entry of list.getEntries()) {
            frames.push(entry.duration);
        }
    });
    observer.observe({ entryTypes: ['longtask'] });
    
    // Simulate fast scroll
    const scrollHeight = container.scrollHeight;
    for (let i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
        container.scrollTop = (scrollHeight / 100) * i;
        await new Promise(r => requestAnimationFrame(r));
    }
    
    observer.disconnect();
    
    const avgFrameTime = frames.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / frames.length;
    return {
        avgFrameTime,
        fps: avgFrameTime ? 1000 / avgFrameTime : 60,
        longTasks: frames.filter(f => f > 50).length,
        smooth: avgFrameTime < 16.67  // 60fps target
    };
}
```

## Performance Optimization Checklist

### Bundle Optimization
- [ ] Code splitting (auth.js loads first, viewer.js lazy-loaded)
- [ ] Tree shaking (unused code removed)
- [ ] Critical CSS inlined in index.html
- [ ] WASM files preloaded via `<link rel="preload">`
- [ ] Brotli compression for JS/CSS (if hosting supports)

### Runtime Optimization
- [ ] Database queries use prepared statements
- [ ] FTS5 queries use LIMIT
- [ ] Virtual scrolling for >100 results
- [ ] Debounced search input
- [ ] Web Worker for all crypto operations

### Memory Optimization
- [ ] withDatabaseScope() pattern used consistently
- [ ] Large result sets streamed (not loaded into memory)
- [ ] OPFS used for database persistence
- [ ] Blob URLs revoked after use

## Exit Criteria
- [ ] Lighthouse performance score ≥85 on simulated 3G
- [ ] First Contentful Paint <2s
- [ ] Argon2 derivation <3s on desktop, <9s on mobile
- [ ] Search latency <100ms for all test queries
- [ ] No memory leaks detected (100 search ops)
- [ ] 60fps scroll performance with virtual list
- [ ] XLarge archive (100K messages) loads and searches correctly

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-euch","title":"P1.4a: Verify Command for CI Pipelines","description":"# P1.4a: Verify Command for CI Pipelines

## Goal
Provide `cass pages --verify` to validate an existing export bundle for CI/CD. The verifier must confirm correct structure, config schema, payload integrity, and the absence of secrets in site/.

## CLI Interface

```
cass pages --verify <PATH>

OPTIONS:
  --json         Output machine-readable JSON
  -v, --verbose  Include detailed check results
```

`PATH` may be either the export root (containing site/) or the site/ directory itself. Verify resolves to the site/ directory.

## Verification Checks

### 1) Required Files
- index.html
- config.json
- sw.js
- viewer.js
- auth.js
- styles.css
- robots.txt
- .nojekyll
- payload/ (with chunk files)
- integrity.json (if present, must validate)

### 2) config.json Schema
Validate required fields and types:
- version, export_id (base64, 16 bytes), base_nonce (base64, 12 bytes)
- algorithm == aes-256-gcm
- compression in {deflate, zstd, none}
- payload.chunk_size <= 32 MiB (default 8 MiB), payload.chunk_count > 0
- payload.files list length == chunk_count
- key_slots[] with slot_type, kdf, salt, nonce, wrapped_dek
- no human labels or PII fields in public config

### 3) Payload Manifest and Size Limits
- payload/chunk-00000.bin ... payload/chunk-N.bin exist and are contiguous
- each chunk file <= 100 MB (GitHub Pages hard limit)
- total site size computed and reported

### 4) integrity.json (if present)
- recompute sha256 for each site/ file
- compare with integrity.json entries
- fail on mismatch; report missing/extra files

### 5) Secret Leakage Checks
- ensure site/ does not contain recovery-secret.txt, qr-code.*, master-key.json, or private/
- ensure config.json does not include secret material (no plaintext passwords, no labels)

### 6) Optional blobs/
- if blobs/ exists, each filename must be sha256-*.bin
- blobs are included in integrity.json

## JSON Output (example)
```json
{
  "status": "valid",
  "checks": {
    "required_files": true,
    "config_schema": true,
    "payload_manifest": true,
    "size_limits": true,
    "integrity": true,
    "no_secrets_in_site": true
  },
  "warnings": [],
  "site_size_bytes": 25678901
}
```

## Test Requirements

### Unit Tests
- Missing required files -> failure
- Invalid config.json fields -> failure
- Chunk count mismatch -> failure
- integrity.json mismatch -> failure

### Integration Tests
- Verify a known-good fixture export passes
- Verify a fixture with private files in site/ fails

### E2E Script
- Build export -> bundle -> cass pages --verify --json
- Log each check clearly with PASS/FAIL and timing

## Files to Create/Modify
- src/pages/verify.rs
- src/cli/pages.rs (add --verify)
- tests/pages_verify.rs
- tests/fixtures/pages_verify/

## Exit Criteria
1. Verify passes on valid bundles and fails on invalid ones
2. JSON output is stable for CI parsing
3. Clear error messages in verbose mode

Support direct URLs to specific conversations, messages, search results, and app panels, enabling shareability and browser history navigation on static hosting.

#/c/12345                -> conversation 12345
#/c/12345/m/67            -> message 67 in conversation 12345

## Router Behavior

2. Share links copy correctly
3. Browser history works
4. Route-based panels render reliably

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-f12dce92","title":"Query Suggestions (did-you-mean)","description":"# Query Suggestions (did-you-mean)

## Problem Statement
When a search returns 0 results, agents have no guidance on what to try next:
- Was it a typo? ("git comit" → "git commit")
- Too specific? (suggest removing terms)
- Wrong field? (content vs title)

## Proposed Solution
Add suggestions to empty/sparse result sets:
```bash
cass search "git comit" --json
```

Output:
```json
{
  "count": 0,
  "hits": [],
  "suggestions": {
    "did_you_mean": ["git commit", "git comment"],
    "similar_terms": ["commit", "git"],
    "try_broader": ["git"],
    "popular_queries": ["git push", "git merge"]
  }
}
```

## Design Decisions

### When to Suggest
- Always when count=0
- Optionally when count < threshold (sparse results)
- Can be disabled with `--no-suggestions` for performance

### Suggestion Types
1. **did_you_mean**: Levenshtein-based typo corrections
2. **similar_terms**: Terms that appear in the index that are similar
3. **try_broader**: Subsets of the query to try
4. **popular_queries**: Common queries in similar domains (optional)

### Implementation Complexity
Requires:
- Index term enumeration (for did_you_mean)
- Edit distance calculation
- Query history tracking (for popular_queries, optional)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Zero-result searches include `suggestions` object
- [ ] `did_you_mean` provides typo corrections
- [ ] `similar_terms` finds related indexed terms
- [ ] `try_broader` suggests query simplifications
- [ ] `--no-suggestions` disables for performance
- [ ] Suggestions are ranked by relevance

## Effort Estimate
Medium-High - 4-6 hours. Requires index term access and string similarity logic.

## Future Enhancement
Could integrate with LLM for semantic suggestions, but that's a separate feature.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","created_at":"2025-11-30T23:54:08.438234185Z","updated_at":"2025-12-01T20:00:37.431959311Z","closed_at":"2025-12-01T20:00:37.431959311Z","compaction_level":0}

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-ifou","title":"P3.1b: Password Strength Meter & Validation","description":"# P3.1b: Password Strength Meter & Validation

**Parent Phase:** Phase 3: Web Viewer
**Section Reference:** Plan Document Section 13, lines 3076-3098
**Depends On:** P3.1 (Authentication UI)

## Goal

Implement real-time password strength validation with visual feedback during both export (CLI wizard) and unlock (web viewer).

## Technical Approach

### Strength Scoring Algorithm (Rust - for CLI)

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum PasswordStrength {
    Weak,
    Fair,
    Good,
    Strong,
}

impl PasswordStrength {
    pub fn color(&self) -> &'static str {
        match self {
            Self::Weak => "red",
            Self::Fair => "yellow",
            Self::Good => "blue",
            Self::Strong => "green",
        }
    }
}

pub fn validate_password(password: &str) -> (PasswordStrength, Vec<&'static str>) {
    let length = password.len();
    let has_upper = password.chars().any(|c| c.is_uppercase());
    let has_lower = password.chars().any(|c| c.is_lowercase());
    let has_digit = password.chars().any(|c| c.is_numeric());
    let has_special = password.chars().any(|c| !c.is_alphanumeric());

    let length_score = match length {
        0..=7 => 0,
        8..=11 => 1,
        12..=15 => 2,
        _ => 3,
    };

    let score = length_score
        + has_upper as u8
        + has_lower as u8
        + has_digit as u8
        + has_special as u8;

    let mut suggestions = Vec::new();
    if length < 12 {
        suggestions.push("Use at least 12 characters");
    }
    if !has_upper {
        suggestions.push("Add uppercase letters");
    }
    if !has_digit {
        suggestions.push("Add numbers");
    }
    if !has_special {
        suggestions.push("Add special characters (!@#$%^&*)");
    }

    let strength = match score {
        0..=2 => PasswordStrength::Weak,
        3..=4 => PasswordStrength::Fair,
        5..=6 => PasswordStrength::Good,
        _ => PasswordStrength::Strong,
    };

    (strength, suggestions)
}
```

### JavaScript Version (for Web Viewer)

```javascript
// web/src/password-strength.js
export function validatePassword(password) {
    const length = password.length;
    const hasUpper = /[A-Z]/.test(password);
    const hasLower = /[a-z]/.test(password);
    const hasDigit = /[0-9]/.test(password);
    const hasSpecial = /[^a-zA-Z0-9]/.test(password);

    let lengthScore = length < 8 ? 0 : length < 12 ? 1 : length < 16 ? 2 : 3;
    let score = lengthScore 
        + (hasUpper ? 1 : 0) 
        + (hasLower ? 1 : 0) 
        + (hasDigit ? 1 : 0) 
        + (hasSpecial ? 1 : 0);

    const suggestions = [];
    if (length < 12) suggestions.push("Use at least 12 characters");
    if (!hasUpper) suggestions.push("Add uppercase letters");
    if (!hasDigit) suggestions.push("Add numbers");
    if (!hasSpecial) suggestions.push("Add special characters");

    const strength = score <= 2 ? 'weak' 
        : score <= 4 ? 'fair' 
        : score <= 6 ? 'good' 
        : 'strong';

    return { strength, score, suggestions };
}

export function getStrengthColor(strength) {
    return {
        weak: '#ef4444',
        fair: '#f59e0b',
        good: '#3b82f6',
        strong: '#22c55e'
    }[strength];
}
```

### UI Component (Web Viewer)

```html
<div class="password-field">
    <input type="password" id="password" autocomplete="new-password">
    <div class="strength-meter">
        <div class="strength-bar" id="strength-bar"></div>
    </div>
    <div class="strength-label" id="strength-label"></div>
    <ul class="suggestions" id="suggestions"></ul>
</div>
```

```css
.strength-meter {
    height: 4px;
    background: #e5e7eb;
    border-radius: 2px;
    margin-top: 8px;
}

.strength-bar {
    height: 100%;
    border-radius: 2px;
    transition: width 0.3s, background-color 0.3s;
}

.strength-bar[data-strength="weak"] { width: 25%; background: #ef4444; }
.strength-bar[data-strength="fair"] { width: 50%; background: #f59e0b; }
.strength-bar[data-strength="good"] { width: 75%; background: #3b82f6; }
.strength-bar[data-strength="strong"] { width: 100%; background: #22c55e; }
```

```javascript
// Real-time validation
passwordInput.addEventListener('input', () => {
    const { strength, suggestions } = validatePassword(passwordInput.value);
    
    strengthBar.dataset.strength = strength;
    strengthLabel.textContent = strength.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + strength.slice(1);
    
    suggestionsEl.innerHTML = suggestions
        .map(s => `<li>${s}</li>`)
        .join('');
});
```

### CLI Progress Display (during wizard)

```rust
fn display_password_strength(term: &Term, password: &str) -> io::Result<()> {
    let (strength, suggestions) = validate_password(password);
    
    let bar = match strength {
        PasswordStrength::Weak => "[█░░░]",
        PasswordStrength::Fair => "[██░░]",
        PasswordStrength::Good => "[███░]",
        PasswordStrength::Strong => "[████]",
    };
    
    term.clear_line()?;
    write!(term, "Strength: {} {}", 
        style(bar).fg(Color::from_str(strength.color()).unwrap()),
        style(format!("{:?}", strength)).bold()
    )?;
    
    if !suggestions.is_empty() {
        writeln!(term)?;
        for suggestion in suggestions {
            writeln!(term, "  • {}", style(suggestion).dim())?;
        }
    }
    
    Ok(())
}
```

## Test Cases

1. Empty password → Weak, all suggestions shown
2. "password" → Weak (no upper, no digit, no special)
3. "Password1!" → Good (all requirements but short)
4. "MySecureP@ssw0rd!" → Strong
5. Unicode characters handled correctly
6. Real-time updates as user types
7. Suggestions disappear as requirements met

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-ifr7","title":"[P0] Opt 2: SIMD Dot Product Implementation","description":"# Optimization 2: SIMD Dot Product Implementation

## Problem Statement

After F16 pre-conversion (Optimization 1), the remaining hotspot is the scalar dot product loop. Even with LLVM's auto-vectorization, explicit SIMD can provide guaranteed vectorization and better instruction scheduling.

### Current Implementation (vector_index.rs:1221-1228)
```rust
fn dot_product(a: &[f32], b: &[f32]) -> f32 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}
```

### Why Explicit SIMD?
- LLVM auto-vectorization is not guaranteed - depends on optimization level, alignment, loop structure
- Explicit SIMD provides predictable, measurable performance
- AVX2 processes 8 floats per instruction (256-bit registers)
- SSE processes 4 floats per instruction (128-bit registers)

## Proposed Solution

Use the `wide` crate for portable SIMD that works across x86_64 (AVX2/SSE) and ARM (NEON).

### Implementation Location
- File: `src/search/vector_index.rs`
- Add new function: `dot_product_simd`
- Modify `dot_product_at` to use SIMD version

### Code Implementation
```rust
use wide::f32x8;

fn dot_product_simd(a: &[f32], b: &[f32]) -> f32 {
    let chunks_a = a.chunks_exact(8);
    let chunks_b = b.chunks_exact(8);
    let remainder_a = chunks_a.remainder();
    let remainder_b = chunks_b.remainder();

    let mut sum = f32x8::ZERO;
    for (ca, cb) in chunks_a.zip(chunks_b) {
        let arr_a: [f32; 8] = ca.try_into().unwrap();
        let arr_b: [f32; 8] = cb.try_into().unwrap();
        sum += f32x8::from(arr_a) * f32x8::from(arr_b);
    }

    let mut scalar_sum: f32 = sum.reduce_add();
    for (a, b) in remainder_a.iter().zip(remainder_b) {
        scalar_sum += a * b;
    }
    scalar_sum
}
```

### Why `wide` Crate?
- Version 0.7.x is stable and maintained
- Provides `f32x8::ZERO` for zero-initialization
- Provides `reduce_add()` for horizontal sum
- Portable across x86_64 and ARM
- No unsafe code in user code

## Isomorphism Note

**Important**: SIMD reorders floating-point operations, causing ~1e-7 relative error in scores.

### Why This Is Acceptable
- **Ranking order is preserved**: Score differences are too small to change ordering
- **Same result set**: Same (message_id, chunk_idx) pairs returned
- **Industry standard**: All vector search engines accept this trade-off

### Verification
```rust
// Property test
for query in test_queries {
    let scalar = dot_product(&a, &b);
    let simd = dot_product_simd(&a, &b);
    assert!((scalar - simd).abs() < 1e-5 * scalar.abs().max(1.0));
}
```

## Pre-Implementation Verification

Before implementing explicit SIMD, verify LLVM isn't already auto-vectorizing:
```bash
RUSTFLAGS="--emit=asm" cargo build --release
# Check target/release/deps/*.s for vmulps/vaddps (AVX) or mulps/addps (SSE)
```

If auto-vectorization is already happening, explicit SIMD may provide marginal benefit. Benchmark to verify.

## Expected Impact

| Metric | Before (post-Opt1) | After |
|--------|-------------------|-------|
| `vector_index_search_50k` | ~30ms | 10-15ms |
| Speedup | Baseline | 2-4x |

## Cargo.toml Addition
```toml
[dependencies]
wide = "0.7"  # Portable SIMD
```

## Rollback Strategy

Environment variable `CASS_SIMD_DOT=0` to:
- Disable SIMD implementation
- Fall back to scalar dot product
- Useful for debugging FP precision issues

## Dependencies

- **Depends on**: Optimization 1 (F16 Pre-Convert) - SIMD on uniform F32 is simpler and faster
- **Blocks**: Optimization 3 (Parallel) - Parallelizing fast SIMD ops yields best results","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"","created_at":"2026-01-10T02:41:48.349307503Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T06:39:02.441031765Z","closed_at":"2026-01-10T06:39:02.441031765Z","close_reason":"Implemented SIMD dot product optimization achieving 2.7x additional speedup (16ms → 6ms). Combined with Opt 1: 16x total speedup (97ms → 6ms)","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-ifr7","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-avt1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-10T03:09:02.672355726Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-ifxo","title":"P6.10: Recovery Testing","description":"# P6.10: Recovery Testing

## Goal
Verify password recovery, key slot rotation, and disaster recovery procedures work correctly, ensuring users can regain access to their data when passwords are forgotten.

## Test Areas

### Recovery Key Flow
- Generate recovery key during export
- Verify recovery key unlocks archive
- Test recovery key format (word-based vs hex)
- Verify recovery works after password change

### Multi-Key-Slot Testing
- Add new key slot to existing archive
- Remove key slot from archive
- Verify all active slots work independently
- Test maximum slot limit

### Disaster Recovery
- Recover from corrupted key slot metadata
- Partial archive recovery (some chunks valid)
- Re-export from partial data
- Backup verification

### Edge Cases
- Recovery with typos (fuzzy matching)
- Case sensitivity in recovery keys
- Special characters in passwords
- Unicode normalization

## Test Implementation

```rust
#[test]
fn test_recovery_key_unlocks() {
    let (archive, recovery_key) = export_with_recovery("password");
    
    // Primary password works
    let decrypted1 = decrypt(&archive, "password").unwrap();
    
    // Recovery key also works
    let decrypted2 = decrypt_with_recovery(&archive, &recovery_key).unwrap();
    
    assert_eq!(decrypted1, decrypted2);
}

#[test]
fn test_add_key_slot() {
    let archive = export_encrypted(&data, "password1");
    
    // Add second password
    let updated = add_key_slot(&archive, "password1", "password2").unwrap();
    
    // Both work
    assert!(decrypt(&updated, "password1").is_ok());
    assert!(decrypt(&updated, "password2").is_ok());
}
```

## Files to Create
- tests/recovery/key_slots.rs
- tests/recovery/disaster.rs
- web/tests/recovery.spec.js
- docs/RECOVERY.md

## Exit Criteria
- [ ] Recovery key generation tested
- [ ] All key slots work independently
- [ ] Slot addition/removal works
- [ ] Recovery procedures documented","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","created_at":"2026-01-07T01:53:51.925169197Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-26T23:35:39.546834408Z","closed_at":"2026-01-26T23:35:39.546834408Z","close_reason":"All 36 recovery tests pass. Fixed compilation errors (type mismatches, BTreeMap iteration), added password validation (reject empty/whitespace). Recovery key generation, multi-key-slot operations, disaster recovery scenarios, and edge cases all tested.  {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-inle","title":"[Task] Opt 7.3: Benchmark SQLite ID caching","description":"# Task: Benchmark SQLite ID Caching

## Objective

Measure query reduction and indexing speedup from ID caching.

## Benchmark Protocol

### 1. Query Count Measurement

Use SQLite profiling to count queries:

```bash
# Without cache
export CASS_SQLITE_CACHE=0
SQLITE_PROFILE=1 cass index --full 2>&1 | grep -c "SELECT\|INSERT"

# With cache
unset CASS_SQLITE_CACHE
SQLITE_PROFILE=1 cass index --full 2>&1 | grep -c "SELECT\|INSERT"
```

### Expected Query Reduction

From PLAN:
- Without cache: 12,000+ queries for 3000 conversations
- With cache: ~200 queries (one per unique agent/workspace)
- **Expected reduction: 60x fewer queries**

### 2. Indexing Throughput Benchmark

```bash
# Baseline
CASS_SQLITE_CACHE=0 cargo bench --bench runtime_perf -- index --save-baseline no_cache

# With cache
cargo bench --bench runtime_perf -- index --save-baseline with_cache

critcmp no_cache with_cache
```

### 3. Cache Hit Rate Measurement

Add instrumentation to track cache hits:

```rust
static CACHE_HITS: AtomicUsize = AtomicUsize::new(0);
static CACHE_MISSES: AtomicUsize = AtomicUsize::new(0);

fn get_or_create_agent_id(&mut self, ...) -> Result<i64> {
    if let Some(&id) = self.agent_cache.get(name) {
        CACHE_HITS.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
        return Ok(id);
    }
    CACHE_MISSES.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
    // ... query database
}
```

### Expected Cache Hit Rate

For typical usage:
- 1 agent (e.g., "claude") for all conversations
- 10-50 workspaces
- 3000 conversations

Hit rate = (3000 - 1) / 3000 = 99.97% for agents
Hit rate = (3000 - 50) / 3000 = 98.3% for workspaces

### 4. Memory Overhead Measurement

Cache memory usage is minimal:
- Agent cache: ~1-5 entries × 50 bytes = < 1 KB
- Workspace cache: ~10-100 entries × 100 bytes = < 10 KB
- Total overhead: < 100 KB

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Query count reduced by 50x+
- [ ] Indexing throughput improves measurably
- [ ] Cache hit rate > 95%
- [ ] Memory overhead < 100 KB
- [ ] Documentation updated with results","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","created_at":"2026-01-10T03:20:55.655004032Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T03:40:19.948475476Z","closed_at":"2026-01-10T03:40:19.948475476Z","close_reason":"Duplicates - consolidated into t330/mbei/16pz/1tmi chain","compaction_level":0}
